planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; woodland sunflower spreads by rhizome to form large colonies and is best used where aggressive naturalization is welcome — it is exceptionally effective at naturalizing along dry woodland edges and under oak canopies.

directions: Sow seed in fall or divide in spring in dry to average, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — woodland sunflower is one of the very few perennial sunflowers genuinely adapted to partial shade, producing 2–3 inch yellow daisy flowers in mid-to-late summer on upright stems 3–6 feet tall in conditions where most sunflowers refuse to bloom. It grows naturally in dry, open woodlands, woodland edges, and clearings throughout the eastern US and is especially common along the edges of oak woodland, thriving in the dry shade beneath the oak canopy that challenges most sun-loving perennials. It spreads vigorously by rhizome — use this to advantage in naturalizing dry, shaded areas with low-maintenance native cover and late-season pollinator value. The seeds are eaten by goldfinches and other seed-eating birds. An outstanding native for filling the challenging dry, part-shade niche.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to moist, deep prairie soil in full sun; prairie blazing star is the tallest of the common blazing stars and requires a site with good moisture in summer to achieve its full 4–5 foot flowering height.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep prairie soil in full sun — prairie blazing star is the most dramatic of the native liatris species, producing the tallest and most densely packed flower spikes — 3–5 feet tall — of dense magenta-rose florets that open from the top of the spike downward (a reversal of the usual bottom-to-top progression) in midsummer. Like all liatris species, the flowers are intensely attractive to monarch butterflies (which use them heavily during fall migration), native bumblebees, and a wide array of specialist prairie bees. It grows naturally in the wet to mesic tallgrass prairies of the Midwest, requiring better soil moisture in summer than the drier-site liatris species. The corms (thickened stem bases) survive prairie fires, drought, and cold with equal ease. An outstanding focal-point perennial for prairie gardens and naturalistic plantings where maximum height and drama are needed.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; yellow giant hyssop is uniquely tolerant of partial shade among the native Agastache species, thriving in the open woodland understory where its relatives cannot grow.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to average, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade — yellow giant hyssop is the woodland-adapted member of the native Agastache genus and the only common native hyssop that thrives in partial shade rather than full sun. It produces the genus's characteristic long, dense, vertical flower spikes covered with tiny two-lipped flowers, but in pale yellow-green to cream rather than the orange or lavender of its more familiar relatives. At 4–6 feet tall, it is one of the most imposing native shade perennials available, providing a strong vertical accent in the woodland garden. Strongly anise-scented throughout. The small flowers provide important nectar for native bees, small butterflies, and particularly for Hemaris moth species (hummingbird moths). Self-sows moderately in appropriate conditions. A striking and underused native for large-scale woodland and shaded native gardens in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; hairy skullcap grows naturally in dry to moist hardwood forest understories and tolerates a range of shade from partial to fairly deep — one of the more adaptable native skullcaps.

directions: Sow seed in fall in dry to moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade — hairy skullcap is a native Scutellaria of eastern deciduous woodland, producing terminal clusters of blue-violet, 3/4-inch, two-lipped flowers on hairy stems 6–16 inches tall from late spring through early summer. The genus name comes from the small, helmet-shaped (scutellum) protrusion on the upper side of the calyx visible in fruit. Native bumblebees are the primary pollinators. Hairy skullcap grows naturally in dry to moist hardwood forest understories from New York south to Florida and west to the Midwest — one of the more shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant native skullcaps, able to perform in the challenging dry-shade conditions under mature deciduous trees. The basal and stem leaves are opposite and elliptic, distinctly hairy. An attractive and ecologically valuable small native for the shaded wildflower garden and native plant border.

planting.note: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to gravelly, thin soil in full sun to partial shade; smooth coneflower is a Federally Threatened species adapted to disturbed, thin-soil openings in cedar-hardwood woodland — it requires good drainage and open mineral soil.

directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to gravelly soil in full sun to partial shade — smooth coneflower is a Federally Threatened native coneflower of the Appalachian piedmont and Blue Ridge foothills, declining primarily due to habitat loss and canopy closure over its characteristic thin-soil, semi-open woodland and roadside habitats. It is distinguished from the more common purple coneflower (E. purpurea) by its smooth (not rough) stems and leaves and its adaptation to disturbed, thin, rocky soils — often in partially shaded situations unlike most other Echinacea. The pale to deep rose-purple ray flowers and prominent central cone are characteristic of the genus and equally attractive to pollinators and beneficial for gardens. Growing this species supports its conservation by maintaining cultivated populations and seed banks. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants from reputable native plant nurseries.
sources: US Fish and Wildlife Service (Threatened Species)
